# UC-22.26.8 · Emergency preparedness evidence

## Description

Ingests drill timelines, inject acknowledgements, and recovery checkpoints as auditable preparedness records.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO KBF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes comms and sourcetypes drill:ack, drill:inject. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=comms`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=drill:ack`, `sourcetype=drill:inject`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=comms sourcetype IN ("drill:inject","drill:ack") earliest=-120d
| stats earliest(eval(if(match(_raw,"inject"),_time,null()))) as t_inject, earliest(eval(if(match(_raw,"ack"),_time,null()))) as t_ack by drill_id
| eval ack_min=round((t_ack-t_inject)/60,1)
| where isnull(t_ack) OR ack_min>15
| table drill_id, t_inject, t_ack, ack_min
```

Understanding this SPL

**Emergency preparedness evidence (Kraftberedskap)** — Ingests drill timelines, inject acknowledgements, and recovery checkpoints as auditable preparedness records.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: comms.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=comms, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **drill_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ack_min** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(t_ack) OR ack_min>15` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Emergency preparedness evidence (Kraftberedskap)** — Ingests drill timelines, inject acknowledgements, and recovery checkpoints as auditable preparedness records.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: ack_min, drill_id, t_ack, t_inject.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the GRC system and the audit register, and with regional reporting cutoffs in your runbook. Document any expected regulatory reporting window or approved exception before escalating.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).

## SPL

```spl
index=comms sourcetype IN ("drill:inject","drill:ack") earliest=-120d
| stats earliest(eval(if(match(_raw,"inject"),_time,null()))) as t_inject, earliest(eval(if(match(_raw,"ack"),_time,null()))) as t_ack by drill_id
| eval ack_min=round((t_ack-t_inject)/60,1)
| where isnull(t_ack) OR ack_min>15
| table drill_id, t_inject, t_ack, ack_min
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations). Key fields: `ack_min`, `drill_id`, `t_ack`, `t_inject`.
